agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A courier-tracking feature is proposed to show customers their courier's real-time location during delivery. Two internal stakeholder teams have submitted directly contradictory requirements: Customer Success demands full GPS transparency as a competitive differentiator, while Operations demands location be hidden entirely due to driver retention problems caused by feelings of surveillance.","bounded_context":"Delivery Tracking / Last-Mile Visibility","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Courier","definition":"A delivery driver employed or contracted to transport packages from origin to customer. A human with privacy rights and retention risk."},{"term":"Real-time location","definition":"The courier's GPS coordinates updated at frequent intervals during an active delivery."},{"term":"Active delivery","definition":"The period from when a courier picks up a package until it is marked as delivered or failed."},{"term":"Customer","definition":"The recipient of a package who has a legitimate interest in knowing delivery status and estimated arrival."},{"term":"Location granularity","definition":"The precision level at which a courier's position is exposed — ranging from exact GPS coordinates to neighborhood-level zones to abstract progress indicators."},{"term":"Delivery ETA","definition":"Estimated time of arrival — the business-relevant information customers actually need, which can be derived from location without exposing location itself."}]},"business_rules":[],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Courier is stationary for extended period (e.g., traffic, break) and customer sees no movement","expected_behavior":"Exact GPS would make customers anxious or lead to complaints; an abstracted view (ETA-based or zone-based) naturally smooths this"},{"scenario":"Courier deviates from expected route (multi-drop, personal errand)","expected_behavior":"Exact GPS exposes routing decisions that confuse or anger customers; abstracted views hide irrelevant intermediate states"},{"scenario":"Courier is near customer address but delivering to a neighbor first","expected_behavior":"Exact GPS showing courier 'right there' but not arriving creates frustration; ETA or stop-count is more honest"},{"scenario":"Post-delivery — customer requests historical courier location data","expected_behavior":"Retention of exact GPS trails creates privacy and legal liability; must define data retention policy"},{"scenario":"Courier opts out of all tracking","expected_behavior":"Business must decide if tracking is a condition of employment/contract or if opt-out is permitted — labor law implications"}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Getting this wrong in either direction has real cost. Losing drivers (Operations concern) increases recruitment and training spend — typically $3K-$5K per driver. Losing customers (Customer Success concern) reduces revenue. The compromise solution (abstracted tracking) preserves both revenue streams.","operational":"Exact GPS tracking at high frequency increases infrastructure cost (location streaming, storage). Abstracted or ETA-based tracking is cheaper to serve and easier to maintain. Driver retention directly impacts operational capacity and delivery SLA adherence.","user":"Customers want predictability, not surveillance capability. Research across the logistics industry (Uber, DoorDash, Amazon) shows customers primarily want to know WHEN, not WHERE exactly. An ETA with a progress indicator satisfies 90%+ of the actual customer need.","risk":"CRITICAL: Exact GPS exposure to customers may violate GDPR (EU), CCPA (California), and equivalent privacy regulations depending on jurisdiction. Courier GPS coordinates are personal data. Sharing them with third parties (customers) requires explicit, informed, freely-given consent from the courier — which is hard to claim is 'freely given' when it is a condition of employment. This is a legal liability that neither team has addressed.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Want delivery predictability. Actual need is ETA and progress, not raw coordinates. A well-designed abstracted view may satisfy them better than a map pin."},{"group":"Couriers/Drivers","impact":"Directly affected by privacy and surveillance concerns. Retention is already suffering. Their consent and comfort level is a hard constraint, not a preference."},{"group":"Customer Success team","impact":"Believes transparency drives NPS and retention. Needs to be shown that abstracted tracking can deliver the same customer satisfaction without the driver cost."},{"group":"Operations team","impact":"Bears the cost of driver churn. Their concern is validated by real attrition data. Needs a solution they can credibly sell to drivers as respectful."},{"group":"Legal/Compliance","impact":"NOT YET CONSULTED but critically affected. GPS data sharing has regulatory implications that could override both teams' preferences."},{"group":"Product/Leadership","impact":"Must arbitrate this conflict. Needs a recommendation that balances both concerns with data."}]},"confidence":{"level":35,"reasoning":"Confidence is very low because this is a direct contradiction between two stakeholder teams, both claiming non-negotiable status, AND there is an unaddressed legal/privacy dimension that could invalidate both positions. No spec can be written until leadership arbitrates. Additionally, Legal/Compliance has not weighed in on what is even permissible.","high_confidence_areas":["The two requirements as stated are mutually exclusive and cannot both be satisfied","There is a well-established industry pattern (abstracted tracking) that likely resolves this","Courier GPS data is personal data under most privacy frameworks","Driver retention is a real operational cost that must be weighed"],"low_confidence_areas":["Which team's requirement takes priority — this is a business leadership decision","Jurisdiction-specific privacy and labor law constraints","Whether drivers have contractual obligations around location sharing","Actual customer satisfaction data — does exact GPS really drive NPS or is that an assumption?"],"assumptions":["Both teams are acting in good faith based on their domain perspective","Driver attrition complaints about surveillance are genuine and documented","Customer Success has not A/B tested exact GPS vs abstracted tracking alternatives","No legal review of GPS data sharing has been conducted yet"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"Customer Success requires showing exact courier GPS coordinates to customers at all times. Operations requires never showing courier location to customers. These requirements are mutually exclusive as stated. Neither team can be satisfied if the other's requirement is implemented verbatim.","affected_stakeholders":["Customer Success","Operations","Couriers","Customers","Legal/Compliance","Leadership"],"options":["A: Implement exact GPS tracking (satisfies Customer Success, violates Operations, risks driver attrition and possible privacy violations)","B: Show no location at all (satisfies Operations, violates Customer Success, removes a competitive feature)","C: Implement abstracted tracking — show ETA, delivery progress, and approximate zone (e.g., '5 minutes away') without exposing exact coordinates. This is the industry-standard compromise used by major logistics platforms.","D: Implement tiered tracking with courier consent — couriers opt into a level of location sharing, customers see what the courier permits"],"recommendation":"Option C (abstracted tracking) resolves the contradiction. Customers get the predictability they actually need (ETA, progress, proximity). Couriers are not surveilled because exact coordinates are never exposed to customers. This is proven at scale by Amazon, UberEats, and DoorDash. However, this recommendation CANNOT be implemented as acceptance criteria until Leadership explicitly arbitrates between the two teams and Legal reviews the privacy implications. Escalate to VP-level decision maker with both teams present."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"Legal and compliance review of courier GPS data handling is entirely absent. Sharing a worker's real-time GPS coordinates with customers is a personal data processing activity under GDPR/CCPA. Before ANY requirement is finalized, Legal must determine what is permissible in each operating jurisdiction.","affected_stakeholders":["Legal/Compliance","Couriers","Leadership"],"options":["Proceed without legal review (high risk)","Block feature until legal review completes (safe but slow)","Implement abstracted tracking which minimizes privacy exposure while legal reviews exact GPS option"],"recommendation":"Do not finalize any requirement until Legal provides guidance. Abstracted tracking (Option C above) is the lowest-risk interim path because it avoids exposing personal data while still delivering customer value."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Contradictory requirements from Customer Success and Operations make it impossible to define correct acceptance criteria","business_rule_broken":"No coherent business rule can encode 'always show exact GPS' AND 'never show location' simultaneously"},{"violation":"Missing legal and privacy review for GPS personal data sharing","business_rule_broken":"Any implementation that shares courier GPS with customers without legal sign-off risks regulatory violation — correctness cannot be determined without this input"}]}

Two internal stakeholder teams have submitted directly contradictory requirements: Customer Success demands full GPS transparency as a competitive differentiator, while Operations demands location be hidden entirely due to driver retention problems caused by feelings of surveillance.","bounded_context":"Delivery Tracking / Last-Mile Visibility","ubi
